epigraph.text: Always being prepared to make a defense to anyone who asks you for a reason for the hope that is in you.

content.text:
Now we learn the formal structure of a geometric proof. A 
**two-column proof** organizes our reasoning 
into a clear, checkable format: statements on the left, justifications 
on the right.
A two-column proof has these components:

items:
**Statements** — a chain of claims leading from 
 Given to Prove

content.title: Two-column proof
content.problem:
**Given:** $M$ is the midpoint of $\overline{AB}$

**Prove:** $AM = \frac{1}{2}AB$

| Statements | Reasons |
| 1. $M$ is the midpoint of $\overline{AB}$ | 1. Given |
| 2. $AM = MB$ | 2. Definition of midpoint |
| 3. $AM + MB = AB$ | 3. Segment Addition Postulate |
| 4. $AM + AM = AB$ | 4. Substitution (from 2) |
| 5. $2 \cdot AM = AB$ | 5. Combine like terms |
| 6. $AM = \frac{1}{2}AB$ | 6. Division Property of Equality |

proof

content.title: Proof with angles
content.problem:
**Given:** $\angle 1$ and $\angle 2$ are complementary; 
$\angle 3$ and $\angle 2$ are complementary

**Prove:** $\angle 1 \cong \angle 3$

proof

| Statements | Reasons |
| 1. $\angle 1$ and $\angle 2$ are complementary | 1. Given |
| 2. $\angle 3$ and $\angle 2$ are complementary | 2. Given |
| 3. $m\angle 1 + m\angle 2 = 90°$ | 3. Definition of complementary |
| 4. $m\angle 3 + m\angle 2 = 90°$ | 4. Definition of complementary |
| 5. $m\angle 1 + m\angle 2 = m\angle 3 + m\angle 2$ | 5. Substitution (Transitive) |
| 6. $m\angle 1 = m\angle 3$ | 6. Subtraction Property of Equality |
| 7. $\angle 1 \cong \angle 3$ | 7. Definition of congruent angles |

proof

content.title: Congruent Complements Theorem
content.content:
If two angles are complementary to the same angle (or to congruent angles), 
then they are congruent.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Proof with supplementary angles
content.problem:
**Given:** $\angle 1$ and $\angle 2$ form a linear pair; 
$\angle 3$ and $\angle 2$ form a linear pair

**Prove:** $\angle 1 \cong \angle 3$

| Statements | Reasons |
| 1. $\angle 1$ and $\angle 2$ form a linear pair | 1. Given |
| 2. $\angle 3$ and $\angle 2$ form a linear pair | 2. Given |
| 3. $\angle 1$ and $\angle 2$ are supplementary | 3. Linear Pair Postulate |
| 4. $\angle 3$ and $\angle 2$ are supplementary | 4. Linear Pair Postulate |
| 5. $m\angle 1 + m\angle 2 = 180°$ | 5. Definition of supplementary |
| 6. $m\angle 3 + m\angle 2 = 180°$ | 6. Definition of supplementary |
| 7. $m\angle 1 = m\angle 3$ | 7. Subtraction Property of Equality |
| 8. $\angle 1 \cong \angle 3$ | 8. Definition of congruent angles |

proof

content.title: Congruent Supplements Theorem
content.content:
If two angles are supplementary to the same angle (or to congruent angles), 
then they are congruent.
content.label: Theorem

items: **Draw a diagram** and mark all given information.
**Identify what you know** and what you need to prove.

**Work both ways:** Start from the Given and think backward 
 from the Prove.

**Ask:** What definitions, postulates, or theorems connect 
 the Given to the Prove?

**Every statement needs a reason.** If you can't justify 
 a step, you can't use it.

**Write clearly.** Someone else should be able to follow 
 your reasoning.

marginNotes.text:
But all things should be done 
decently and in order.
